questions.stem: A neuropsychologist administers the WAIS-IV to a cognitively healthy 72-year-old woman. Which index score pattern is MOST consistent with the "classic aging pattern"?
questions.explanation: The 'classic aging pattern' on the WAIS involves verbal abilities (VCI, reflecting crystallized intelligence) remaining strong while performance/nonverbal abilities (PRI, reflecting fluid intelligence) and especially processing speed (PSI) decline. Option B shows VCI highest and PSI lowest, consistent with this pattern. Option A shows PSI highest, the opposite of the aging pattern. Option C shows VCI lowest, which contradicts the well-preserved crystallized intelligence finding. Option D shows a flat profile with no differential aging effects.
questions.kn_explanation: The question asks about normal age-related cognitive changes in a healthy 72-year-old, which is normal development across the lifespan (fluid vs crystallized intelligence aging pattern).
questions.quality_comment: Requires applied knowledge of the classic aging pattern with realistic WAIS-IV score profiles. Well-supported by lesson content.
questions.lessonExcerpt: This creates the "classic aging pattern". Verbal abilities stay strong while performance (nonverbal) abilities decline earlier.

questions.stem: Research on the predictive validity of IQ scores for academic achievement indicates that the correlation between IQ and academic performance is BEST characterized as:
options: Strongest at the graduate school level, where cognitive demands are highest
Roughly equivalent across all levels of education from elementary through graduate school
Strongest at the elementary school level and decreasing at higher levels of education
Negligible at all levels, with motivation being the primary predictor of academic success
questions.answer: Strongest at the elementary school level and decreasing at higher levels of education
questions.explanation: IQ scores are the best single predictor of academic achievement, with correlations highest at the elementary school level (approximately .60-.70) and decreasing at higher educational levels (approximately .40-.50 in college, and lower in graduate school). This decline occurs due to restriction of range — students at higher educational levels represent an increasingly select and homogeneous group in terms of cognitive ability. Option A reverses the actual pattern. Option B incorrectly suggests uniform correlations. Option D underestimates IQ's predictive power, which is well-established across educational settings.
questions.kn_explanation: IQ-academic achievement correlations across education levels relate to the influence of individual-environment interaction on development over time (KN22).
questions.quality_comment: Tests a classic psychometric finding about restriction of range effects on IQ-achievement correlations. The lesson files do not explicitly cover this gradient across education levels.
questions.suggestedParagraph: ## The Flynn Effect: Why Test Norms Matter
questions.lessonExcerpt: The CogAT7 predicts academic performance and identifies gifted/at-risk students in grades 2-12
